message.content:
You are verifier #2, independent of all other verifiers. Do NOT confirm findings optimistically — only confirm if you can independently verify the issue exists in the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.

Finding F-022: "run_json overall deadline bounds only backoff sleeps, not actual LLM call time"
File: src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py (line 608)
Description: RUN_JSON_DEADLINE enforced only during backoff sleep intervals, not in-progress backend calls. Worst case: deadline + retries * per-call-timeout far exceeds documented deadline.
PoC: ASSAY_BULK_TIMEOUT=120, ASSAY_REASONING_RETRIES=2, ASSAY_JSON_REROLLS=2, ASSAY_RUN_JSON_DEADLINE=10. Backend stalls for 130s per call. run_json takes ~390s despite 10s deadline.

Instructions:
1. Read the actual file at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py
2. Look at the code at line 608 and surrounding context
3. Independently verify whether the described problem EXISTS in the current code
4. Check if the PoC is reproducible from the actual code as written
5. Consider whether this is already fixed (pass-1 #101-#122, pass-2 #124-#156 fixed many issues)
6. Return your independent verdict

Confirmed = true ONLY if: (a) the code problem exists as described, (b) the PoC is reproducible from current code, (c) it is a genuine production-readiness concern. Set confirmed=false for speculative issues, issues already fixed, or issues where the PoC does not match what the code actually does.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

589	    def run_json(self, request: ReasoningRequest) -> Any:
590	        """Generate and parse JSON, re-rolling on parse failure.
591	
592	        If the caller supplies ``params["json_schema"]``, the BULK tier uses Ollama's
593	        schema-constrained decoding (the reliable path). Otherwise it falls back to loose JSON
594	        mode + prompt instruction. BULK decoding can be deterministic (temp 0), so re-issuing the
595	        identical prompt would yield a byte-identical bad reply; each retry **varies the seed**
596	        (forcing a different deterministic decode) and *monotonically* raises temperature toward
597	        1.0 — never lowering it, so re-rolls never sharpen back toward the same output. ``params``
598	        is frozen, so each attempt builds a fresh request (issue #R6).
599	        """
600	        base_temp = float(request.params.get("temperature", 0.0))
601	        system = request.params.get("system")
602	        json_system = (
603	            f"{system}\n\nRespond with ONLY a single JSON value, no prose."
604	            if system
605	            else "Respond with ONLY a single JSON value, no prose."
606	        )
607	        last: ReasoningError | None = None
608	        deadline = time.monotonic() + RUN_JSON_DEADLINE  # overall bound across all re-rolls (#102)
609	        with _span(
610	            "reasoning.run_json",
611	            {"assay.tier": request.tier.value, "assay.purpose": request.purpose},
612	        ):
613	            for attempt in range(JSON_REROLLS + 1):
614	                if attempt > 0 and time.monotonic() >= deadline:
615	                    raise last or ReasoningError("run_json exceeded its overall deadline")
616	                temp = min(1.0, base_temp + 0.2 * attempt)  # monotonic, never decreases (M3)
617	                params = dict(request.params)
618	                params.update(temperature=temp, system=json_system, _json_mode=True, _seed=attempt)
619	                attempt_req = ReasoningRequest(
620	                    prompt=request.prompt,
621	                    tier=request.tier,
622	                    purpose=request.purpose,
623	                    params=params,
624	                )
625	                # A transient/rate/permanent error is already handled (and bounded by the shared
626	                # deadline) inside _run_with_retries — let it PROPAGATE rather than re-rolling, so
627	                # the budgets do NOT multiply (#102). We re-roll ONLY on a JSON *parse* failure.
628	                text = _run_with_retries(attempt_req, deadline=deadline)
629	                try:
630	                    return extract_json(text)
631	                except ReasoningError as exc:  # parse failure → re-roll with a new seed
632	                    last = exc
633	            raise last if last is not None else ReasoningError("run_json failed")

505	    """Sleep ``secs``, but never past ``deadline`` — so backoff can't blow an overall budget.
506

220	def _with_timeout(fn: Callable[[], Any], timeout: float, what: str) -> Any:
221	    """Run a sync callable with a hard wall-clock timeout via the bounded pool."""
222	    future = _submit_bounded(fn)
223	    try:
224	        return future.result(timeout=timeout)
225	    except FuturesTimeout as exc:
226	        # No future.cancel(): a ThreadPoolExecutor cannot interrupt a thread already blocked
227	        # in a backend call. The worker keeps its slot until the call returns; the bounded
228	        # pool's saturation guard is the real protection against leaked/hung slots.
229	        raise ReasoningError(f"{what} timed out after {timeout:.0f}s") from exc

content.text:
Now I have enough to make a determination. The key question is whether the deadline is enforced during the actual LLM call inside `_attempt`, not just during backoff sleeps.

The code flow is:
1. `run_json` sets `deadline = time.monotonic() + RUN_JSON_DEADLINE` (line 608)
2. The deadline check at line 614 only happens at the **start** of each attempt loop iteration (before the call), not while a call is in-progress
3. `_run_with_retries` receives the deadline but only passes it to `_sleep_bounded` — which only bounds the backoff sleep time
4. `_attempt` calls `_with_timeout(fn, BULK_TIMEOUT + 10, ...)` — using `BULK_TIMEOUT + 10` (130s with default BULK_TIMEOUT=120), NOT the remaining deadline time
5. The thread pool future's `result(timeout=...)` uses the per-call timeout, not the overall deadline

The finding is accurate: the deadline is only enforced during backoff sleep intervals via `_sleep_bounded`, not during the actual in-progress LLM call. The actual call timeout is always `BULK_TIMEOUT + 10` (or `HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT + 30`), regardless of how much time remains on the overall deadline.

With ASSAY_RUN_JSON_DEADLINE=10, ASSAY_BULK_TIMEOUT=120, ASSAY_JSON_REROLLS=2:
- Attempt 0: `_attempt` runs with timeout of 130s. Backend stalls 130s. Returns (or times out at 130s).
- Deadline check at attempt 1 start: 130s > 10s, so raises. BUT this doesn't help — the first call alone already blew the deadline.

The PoC scenario (backend stalls 130s per call, 3 attempts = 390s despite 10s deadline) is confirmed by the code structure. Even with just 1 attempt, it stalls for 130s instead of 10s.

input.reasoning: The deadline is only enforced during backoff sleep intervals via _sleep_bounded (lines 534, 539), not during in-progress LLM calls. The actual per-call timeout passed to _with_timeout (line 477) is always BULK_TIMEOUT + 10 (130s with default settings), regardless of remaining deadline time. The deadline check in run_json (line 614) only fires at the start of each loop iteration — after the prior call has already completed or timed out. A single stalled backend call with BULK_TIMEOUT=120 will block for 130s even if RUN_JSON_DEADLINE=10. With ASSAY_JSON_REROLLS=2 and the backend stalling each call, total time far exceeds the documented deadline. The PoC is reproducible from the current code as written.
